text: The surgeon enters the lesser sac and divides the short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer. The liver is retracted with a Penrose drain. A 3-0 Vicryl suture is placed on the posterior stomach for external retraction, aiding in the exposure of the splenic hilum. The posterior stomach is then dissected.

The surgeon focuses on the inferior pancreas and spleen, dividing the splenocleidomastoid ligament. The inferior border of the pancreas is defined and dissected, and the mental branch from the splenic artery is divided.
Following the division of the omentum, the surgeon places an additional suture on the posterior stomach to enhance exposure of the pancreatic neck.

The surgeon uses Mehron bipolar forceps to carefully expose the middle colic vein, directing attention towards the superior mesenteric vein. The instrument facilitates precise dissection of small pancreatic branches.
The surgeon dissects the superior portion of the pancreas and removes lymph nodes from the common hepatic artery. The pancreas is then tunneled and divided using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon carefully dissects and ligates the dorsal pancreatic artery at the root of the splenic artery. The splenic artery is encircled and clamped, while fluorescent angiography confirms the preservation of hepatic artery flow.
The surgeon clips and divides the splenic artery, followed by the division of the splenic vein using a stapler.

The surgeon observes fibrosis behind the splenic artery and proceeds to dissect the deeper layer of the retroperitoneum. This dissection exposes the aorta and the left adrenal vein, allowing for sampling of the retroperitoneal lymph node at that location. The dissection is completed by following the retroperitoneal fascial plane.
The surgeon places a 19 French Blake drain at the surgical site, completing the procedure.
